title: Structure theorems for intertwining wave operators
speaker: Wilhelm Schlag
affiliation: University of Chicago; Visiting Professor, School of Mathematics
abstract: We will describe an implementation of the Wiener theorem in $L^1$ type convolution algebras in the setting of spectral theory. In joint work with Marius Beceanu we obtained a structure theorem for the wave operators by this method.

proof_stages.label: Wave operators and intertwining
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines wave operators by comparing free and perturbed Schrödinger evolutions, establishes the strong-limit question, and records the intertwining relation that transfers functional calculus of the free Laplacian to the perturbed Hamiltonian.

proof_stages.label: Resolvent and zero-energy regularity framework
proof_stages.summary: The symmetric resolvent identity shifts the problem to invertibility of a Birman-Schwinger type operator, and the Yajima Lp theory is placed in the setting where zero energy must be neither an eigenvalue nor a resonance.

proof_stages.label: Born-Dyson expansion and summability obstacle
proof_stages.summary: A time-independent expansion of the wave operator gives an infinite Born/Dyson series that converges in norm for small scaling-critical L^{3/2}-type potentials but not for large potentials, motivating a nonperturbative summation method.

proof_stages.label: Explicit first Born structure
proof_stages.summary: Fourier transformation and time integration of the first Born term produce singular resolvent denominators, whose kernel can be rewritten as a superposition of translations and reflections with a measure-valued coefficient controlling Lp bounds.

proof_stages.label: Full structure theorem and nonlinear bounds
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts that under V in a B^{1+}-type decay space and regular zero energy, the full wave operators have the same reflection-translation structure as the first term, with a finite coefficient norm depending nonlinearly on the potential and the Birman-Schwinger inverse.

proof_stages.label: Wiener inversion as divergent-series summation
proof_stages.summary: The scalar Wiener lemma is re-proved by frequency localization and then lifted to an operator-valued algebra where translation continuity and vanishing at infinity replace smallness and permit inversion.

proof_stages.label: Custom algebra and twisted Birman-Schwinger inversion
proof_stages.summary: A bespoke kernel algebra Y, with a nonstandard convolution encoding the higher Born terms, is used to formulate a twisted Birman-Schwinger element whose Wiener-algebra inverse lies in Y and therefore yields the full structure formula, followed by a scaling-invariant small-potential variant and open problems.

examples.note: The comparison between straight-line motion for V=0 and nontrapped perturbed trajectories motivates the quantum definition of wave operators and asymptotic completeness.

examples.name: Inverse-square critical scaling
examples.note: The potential with |x|^{-2} scaling explains why L^{3/2} and later B^{1/2}-type norms are the scaling-critical targets for Schrödinger wave-operator estimates.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives the inverse-square criticality from Schrödinger scaling and connects it concretely to the weak L^{3/2} norm and Dyson-series smallness

passes.reason: the inverse-square/L^{3/2} scale is exactly scaling-critical for Schrödinger, introduced as the natural balance rather than as a refutation
the inverse-square decay is the scaling-critical boundary where the potential balances the Laplacian

examples.name: Beceanu-Goldberg large-potential dispersive estimate
examples.note: The earlier use of an operator-valued Wiener algebra to remove a small global-Kato-norm assumption illustrates the inversion strategy later adapted to the structure theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the Beceanu-Goldberg estimate through the Wiener-algebra setup, Fourier transforming the Birman-Schwinger term to a sphere measure and identifying the Kato norm condition

passes.reason: the Beceanu–Goldberg theorem gives the sharp |t|^{-3/2} three-dimensional dispersive rate for large potentials as a positive application rather than an obstruction
